records.search_text: Independent lower-middle-market manufacturers of electric luminaires and fixtures principally designed for commercial, industrial, and institutional buildings, including troffers, linear ambient fixtures, high- and low-bay luminaires, architectural fixtures, task and specialty building fixtures, and integrated LED and control-ready products. Project, specification, distributor, OEM, retrofit, and replacement revenue from electrical distributors, contractors, architects and engineers, facility owners, industrial operators, institutions, energy-service companies, and public agencies. Repeat demand reflects specifications, product families, renovation cycles, warranties, controls compatibility, and installed-base replacement.

records.title: Electric Lamp Bulb and Other Lighting Equipment Manufacturing
records.search_text: US lower-middle-market manufacturers of electric lamp bulbs and parts other than LED semiconductor devices, street and outdoor lighting equipment, flashlights, and other lighting equipment that repeatedly supply external OEM, municipal, distributor, private-label, and contract-manufacturing customers. Predominantly per-unit or project product sales through distributors, OEMs, government buyers, and retailers; qualifying outsourced-service revenue is limited to recurring private-label, contract manufacturing, configuration, and lifecycle support.

records.title: Battery Manufacturing
records.search_text: US lower-middle-market independent manufacturers supplying specialty, industrial, stationary, motive-power, reserve-power, replacement, and configured battery cells, packs, or systems with repeat external customers and support needs. Specialized batteries and systems are sold through repeat OEM, distributor, fleet, defense, medical, data-center, telecom, utility, and industrial relationships, with replacement cycles, configuration, chargers, monitoring, spares, and aftermarket support.

records.title: Automobile and Light Duty Motor Vehicle Manufacturing
records.search_text: U.S. lower-middle-market firms, if any, providing repeat outsourced contract manufacturing of complete automobiles, light-duty motor vehicles, or chassis to external vehicle brands or program owners. Multi-period B2B contract-manufacturing programs with tooling, qualification, launch, assembly, testing, and delivery obligations; revenue would derive from program units, engineering changes, and associated manufacturing support rather than retail vehicle branding.

records.title: Household Furniture (except Wood and Upholstered) Manufacturing
records.search_text: US lower-middle-market manufacturers of nonupholstered household furniture made primarily from metal, plastics, reed, rattan, wicker, fiberglass, or other nonwood materials, supplying external retailers, wholesalers, designers, hospitality accounts, private-label customers, and direct consumers on repeat programs. Repeat product sales through wholesale, retail, private-label, contract, e-commerce, and direct channels, generally priced by SKU or collection with promotions, freight, returns, markdowns, warranties, and periodic line reviews.

records.title: Clothing and Clothing Accessories Merchant Wholesalers
records.search_text: Independent US clothing and clothing-accessories merchant wholesalers that repeatedly source, hold title to, merchandise, sell, finance, and distribute apparel and accessories to retailers and other business customers, with normalized EBITDA of roughly $1-10 million. Repeat B2B merchandise sales earn a resale spread while the distributor supplies assortment selection, line presentation, product content, inventory availability, trade credit, order handling, fulfillment, and returns coordination.

records.title: Motorcycle, ATV, and All Other Motor Vehicle Dealers
records.search_text: US lower-middle-market full-service powersports dealers selling motorcycles, scooters, ATVs, side-by-sides, personal watercraft, snowmobiles, powered golf carts, and closely related vehicles while also supplying repeat repair, maintenance, parts, accessories, warranty, financing, insurance, or trade-in services to external customers. Primarily consumer vehicle sales with repeat ownership-lifecycle revenue from parts, accessories, service labor, repair, warranty administration, financing and insurance commissions, apparel, and future trade-ins. Unit sales are transactional and seasonal; service and ownership support form the recurring outsourced-service component.

records.title: Automotive Parts and Accessories Retailers
records.search_text: US lower-middle-market automotive parts retailers with recurring commercial accounts that provide repair shops, dealers, fleets, and service stations with parts identification and fitment support, quoting, commercial credit, rapid order fulfillment, returns handling, and local delivery. Repeat B2B sales of replacement parts and accessories to professional repair and fleet customers, supported by account-specific pricing and credit, fitment advice, inventory availability, frequent delivery routes, and returns. Product margin funds the bundled procurement and fulfillment service; explicit recurring subscriptions are uncommon.

records.title: Tire Dealers
records.search_text: Independent and regional tire dealers in the lower-middle-market band that repeatedly sell, fit, balance, rotate, inspect, and repair tires for external retail or fleet customers, including locations that also provide adjacent automotive repair. Repeat consumer and fleet visits produce transaction revenue from tires plus installation, balancing, rotation, repair, alignment, and related service labor; some fleet relationships are account-based, but most consumer work is episodic rather than contracted.

records.title: Hardware Retailers
records.search_text: Independent and regional hardware retailers in the lower-middle-market band that repeatedly supply tools, builders' hardware, repair inputs, product advice, order preparation, pickup, and delivery to local households, tradespeople, and small commercial accounts. Predominantly merchandise margin on repeat consumer and professional purchases, supplemented in some stores by delivery, key cutting, rental, repair, account credit, and other services; prices are generally transactional rather than contracted cost-plus fees.

records.title: Other Building Material Dealers
records.search_text: Independent and regional lower-middle-market specialty building-material dealers that repeatedly supply contractors and small builders with quoted material packages, trade credit, product expertise, order staging, pickup, and scheduled delivery, including lumberyards and specialized door, window, cabinet, flooring, fencing, masonry, plumbing, and electrical-supply retailers. Predominantly product gross margin from repeat professional accounts, with value embedded in takeoff and quoting support, account credit, job-lot preparation, delivery sequencing, returns, and product expertise; revenue is usually transactional or account-priced rather than a separately billed service fee.

records.title: Outdoor Power Equipment Retailers
records.search_text: Independent and regional lower-middle-market outdoor power equipment dealers with repeat commercial, municipal, or residential customers and meaningful parts, warranty, diagnostic, repair, maintenance, pickup/delivery, or fleet-support activity alongside equipment sales. Equipment and replacement-unit gross margin, recurring parts and accessories, repair labor, warranty reimbursement, and sometimes rentals, delivery, financing, and fleet support; commercial landscapers and municipalities may buy repeatedly while residential equipment sales are more episodic.

records.title: Nursery, Garden Center, and Farm Supply Retailers
records.search_text: Independent nursery, garden-center, and farm-supply retailers in the lower middle market that provide external commercial customers with recurring procurement, account management, scheduled replenishment, delivery coordination, or comparable repeat outsourced fulfillment. Primarily recurring B2B accounts for landscapers, farms, property operators, and institutional buyers, monetized through merchandise margins plus delivery or account-service fees where charged; contracts are generally short and switching costs are modest.

records.title: Convenience Retailers
records.search_text: Lower-middle-market convenience retailers without fuel pumps that provide recurring external customers with a genuine outsourced food-provision function, such as contracted workplace, institutional, or multi-site pantry stocking, standing-order preparation, or scheduled delivery tied to ongoing account responsibility. Recurring commercial or institutional accounts pay merchandise margins and, where applicable, delivery or account fees for repeated stocking or standing-order fulfillment; consumer foot traffic may coexist but does not itself qualify.

records.title: Vending Machine Operators
records.search_text: Lower-middle-market U.S. vending-machine operators that repeatedly place, stock, merchandise, collect from, monitor, and service machines at external customer locations under route or location relationships. Operators earn merchandise gross margin from repeated end-user purchases and commonly secure continuing access to workplaces, factories, schools, healthcare, hospitality, residential, or public locations through service agreements or location commissions; route density and replenishment quality drive economics.

records.title: Meat Retailers
records.search_text: US lower-middle-market meat retailers with transferable store operations, repeat external customers, accountable procurement and cold-chain handling, and in-store or centrally managed cutting, packaging, merchandising, and fulfillment. Transaction-priced meat and related food sales to repeat households and some foodservice customers, through butcher shops, specialty meat markets, pickup, delivery, and related retail channels.

records.title: Fish and Seafood Retailers
records.search_text: US lower-middle-market fish and seafood retailers with transferable store operations, repeat external customers, accountable sourcing and cold-chain handling, and in-store or centrally managed receiving, inspection, cutting, packaging, merchandising, and fulfillment. Transaction-priced fresh, frozen, and prepared seafood sales to repeat households and some foodservice customers through specialty markets, pickup, delivery, and related retail channels.

records.title: All Other Specialty Food Retailers
records.search_text: US 445298 retailers in the lower-middle-market EBITDA band that provide recurring scheduled replenishment, subscription, direct delivery, or account-managed supply of packaged coffee, tea, bottled water or soft drinks, dairy, spices, gourmet foods, frozen meal plans, or other qualifying specialty foods to external households, businesses, hospitality, or institutions. The coherent retained model earns fixed-price product, delivery, and program revenue from subscriptions, standing orders, scheduled routes, or managed accounts, with sourcing, replenishment, fulfillment, and exception resolution embedded in the margin.

records.title: Beer, Wine, and Liquor Retailers
records.search_text: US lower-middle-market operators of beer, wine, and liquor retail stores that repeatedly provide consumers with licensed product access, assortment, advice, order fulfillment, and related retail service. Predominantly repeat consumer transactions at posted retail prices, with gross profit earned as the spread between merchandise sales and product cost; delivery, events, and memberships are secondary for most operators.

records.title: Furniture Retailers
records.search_text: US lower-middle-market furniture retailers that repeatedly provide external household or business customers with product discovery, design and selection help, ordering, financing coordination, delivery, assembly, installation, and after-sale service through stores or an integrated omnichannel model. Mostly episodic consumer and small-business purchases at negotiated or posted merchandise prices, with gross profit from product markup; design advice, financing, delivery, assembly, and installation support conversion and repeat/referral demand and may be bundled or separately charged.

records.title: Floor Covering Retailers
records.search_text: US lower-middle-market floor-covering retailers that repeatedly serve external residential, builder, property-manager, or commercial customers through product selection, estimating, ordering, delivery, installation coordination, installation, repair, and after-sale accountability. Project-based retail sales of flooring materials, frequently combined with measurement, estimating, delivery, installation, and repair; residential remodel is prominent, and operators generally quote a fixed project price with deposits and final payment tied to installation.

records.title: Window Treatment Retailers
records.search_text: Independent window-treatment retailers in the lower middle market that repeatedly provide externally accountable specification, in-home or on-site measurement, product selection, project coordination, installation, and post-installation support to property managers, designers, builders, or repeat residential customers. Mostly fixed-price projects combining product markup with design, measurement, installation, and support; recurring economics arise from repeat properties, trade-referral relationships, replacements, and service calls rather than subscriptions.

records.title: All Other Home Furnishings Retailers
records.search_text: Lower-middle-market custom picture-framing retailers that repeatedly provide design, conservation-oriented specification, fabrication, mounting, delivery, installation, and project coordination to galleries, designers, artists, hospitality operators, corporate art programs, museums, and other repeat external clients. Fixed-price custom projects combining materials markup with design and skilled fabrication labor; repeat revenue comes from professional trade, gallery, hospitality, corporate, and institutional accounts rather than subscriptions.

records.title: Electronics and Appliance Retailers
records.search_text: Lower-middle-market household-appliance retailers that repeatedly provide externally accountable product specification, delivery, installation, repair, warranty coordination, and ongoing support to property managers, builders, hospitality operators, senior-living operators, and repeat households. Product sales and margin combined with fixed-fee delivery, installation, repair, service plans, parts, and warranty reimbursement; recurrence comes from installed-base failures, portfolio replacements, builder turns, and multi-property customer relationships.

records.title: Department Stores
records.search_text: US department-store operators in the lower-middle-market EBITDA band that repeatedly retail broad lines of merchandise and associated shopping, fulfillment, return, and customer-support services to external consumers. Predominantly consumer merchandise sales at posted or promoted prices, with repeat but usually non-contractual purchases; ancillary delivery, licensed-department, marketplace, media, and credit-card-related income may supplement merchandise margin.

records.title: Warehouse Clubs and Supercenters
records.search_text: US warehouse-club and supercenter operators in the lower-middle-market EBITDA band that repeatedly sell broad lines of merchandise, including substantial grocery assortments, to external consumers or business members. High-volume merchandise sales at low posted prices, often supplemented by annual membership fees, fuel, pharmacy or optical services, advertising, fulfillment, and other ancillary income; purchases repeat frequently but merchandise sales are generally not contractual.

records.title: All Other General Merchandise Retailers
records.search_text: US lower-middle-market operators of fixed-location general, variety, and small-box value stores that repeatedly retail broad lines of new merchandise and associated checkout, fulfillment, return, and customer-support services to external consumers. Mostly repeat but non-contractual consumer merchandise transactions at posted or promoted prices, often emphasizing convenience, value, household essentials, and limited assortments across many categories; ancillary delivery, advertising, or other services may supplement merchandise margin.

records.title: Pharmacies and Drug Retailers
records.search_text: US independent and regional community-pharmacy operators that repeatedly dispense prescriptions and provide medication, immunization, adherence, long-term-care, compounding, or related services to external patients and payers, with normalized EBITDA of roughly $1-10 million. Recurring prescription dispensing and clinical services are paid through PBM or government-program reimbursement, patient copays and cash payments; front-end merchandise is transactional and ancillary.

records.title: Cosmetics, Beauty Supplies, and Perfume Retailers
records.search_text: US independent and regional specialty beauty retailers in the $1-10 million normalized-EBITDA band whose external customers repeatedly use product-replenishment, professional beauty-supply, consultation, loyalty, omnichannel fulfillment, or in-store beauty-service offerings. Consumers and beauty professionals buy replenishment and discretionary products at posted retail prices; qualifying operators add repeat advisory, loyalty, ordering, delivery, education, or in-store services, typically paid per transaction rather than under long-term contracts.

records.title: Optical Goods Retailers
records.search_text: US independent and regional optical retailers with roughly $1-10 million normalized EBITDA that repeatedly dispense, fit, adjust, repair, warrant, replenish, or otherwise service prescription eyewear and contact lenses for external customers and vision-plan members. Customers and managed-vision-care payers fund prescription glasses, contacts and related services through retail purchases, insurance allowances, copays, exams where ancillary, warranties and multi-year eye-care clubs; relationships recur with prescription updates, replacement and replenishment.

records.title: Food (Health) Supplement Retailers
records.search_text: U.S. lower-middle-market food-supplement retailers only where an operating company provides an externally purchased, repeat service around the merchandise, such as subscription replenishment, personalized product curation, adherence support, or managed wellness-product programs. Included firms earn primarily fixed-price product revenue from consumers or organizational customers, sometimes through subscriptions or memberships; the lens recognizes only the accompanying recurring curation, support, or managed-replenishment activity as the outsourced service.

records.title: All Other Health and Personal Care Retailers
records.search_text: U.S. lower-middle-market home and durable medical equipment retailers that repeatedly serve external patients or caregivers through covered-supply refills, equipment rental, delivery and setup, training, maintenance, repair, documentation, and care-team coordination. Included firms receive recurring Medicare, other payer, and patient payments under fee schedules, rental periods, and refill transactions; revenue bundles equipment or consumables with required ordering, documentation, delivery, setup, support, maintenance, and claims work.

records.title: Gasoline Stations with Convenience Stores
records.search_text: U.S. lower-middle-market gasoline stations with convenience stores that also provide a substantive repeat automotive inspection, maintenance, and repair service to external vehicle owners or fleets. Included firms combine high-frequency retail fuel and convenience sales with externally purchased repeat automotive service, typically billed as flat-rate or actual-time labor plus parts and sometimes through fleet-account arrangements.

records.title: Other Gasoline Stations
records.search_text: Lower-middle-market operators of gasoline stations without convenience stores, truck stops, and marine service stations whose operating model includes a recurring external-customer service component such as repair and maintenance or managed fleet fueling accounts. Retail fuel and ancillary products are sold per transaction at posted prices; the eligible subset also earns repeat repair labor and parts revenue or contract/account-based fleet-fueling revenue from external customers.

records.title: Fuel Dealers
records.search_text: Lower-middle-market U.S. dealers delivering heating oil, liquefied petroleum gas, and other fuels repeatedly to external residential, commercial, industrial, agricultural, and governmental customers, including associated account management and equipment service. Dealers earn per-gallon product margin through scheduled, automatic, or on-demand delivery and may also earn recurring service-contract, installation, repair, equipment-rental, budget-plan, and account fees; customers range from households to commercial and agricultural accounts.

records.title: Clothing and Clothing Accessories Retailers
records.search_text: Lower-middle-market clothing and accessories retailers with a material recurring external-customer service component, such as basic alterations and fitting, repeat personal wardrobe or styling service, or recurring school/work uniform programs, alongside sales of new apparel and accessories. The eligible subset earns merchandise margin plus repeat service or program revenue from consumers, schools, employers, or other external customers through fitting, basic alterations, curated wardrobe assistance, and recurring uniform replenishment.

records.title: Shoe Retailers
records.search_text: Lower-middle-market shoe retailers whose customer proposition includes a repeat, externally supplied service component, such as institutional footwear programs, recurring fitting and measurement, or managed replenishment tied to footwear sales. Households or institutional accounts pay mainly per footwear purchase; the eligible subset embeds fitting, selection, account administration, and replenishment in the merchandise margin rather than charging a standalone recurring fee.

records.title: Jewelry Retailers
records.search_text: Lower-middle-market jewelry retailers with a material repeat service offering to external customers, including jewelry and watch repair, resizing and alteration, cleaning and maintenance, appraisal, gem identification, custom design, and related customer-account work combined with new-jewelry retail. Households pay per repair, appraisal, custom project, or merchandise purchase; service-led retailers may generate repeated visits over an item's life and use repairs and maintenance to support long-term trust and merchandise sales.

records.title: Luggage and Leather Goods Retailers
records.search_text: Lower-middle-market luggage and leather-goods retailers with a material repeat external service offering, such as managed corporate luggage programs, repeat warranty administration, personalization, maintenance coordination, or repair intake combined with new-product retail. Consumers or organizational accounts pay mainly per product, with the small eligible subset also charging or embedding fees and margin for repeat personalization, warranty, maintenance coordination, repair intake, or managed replenishment.

records.title: Sporting Goods Retailers
records.search_text: Lower-middle-market sporting-goods firms whose external-customer proposition includes repeat team or institutional supply, equipment fitting, decoration, repair, maintenance, or rental services alongside merchandise. Repeat sales and service to schools, leagues, clubs, institutions, and consumers, generally through merchandise margin plus service, rental, repair, fitting, or decoration fees; contracts and standing accounts may exist but much revenue remains order-based.

records.title: Hobby, Toy, and Game Retailers
records.search_text: Lower-middle-market hobby, toy, and game retailers with repeat external-customer services such as paid memberships, grading or authentication intermediation, repair, rental, instruction, institutional programs, or organized-play operations supported by recurring fees or repeat service purchases. Consumer, collector, club, school, and institutional relationships combining merchandise margin with memberships, grading or authentication fees, repair or rental charges, instruction, events, or repeat program supply; much product revenue remains transactional and seasonal.

records.title: Sewing, Needlework, and Piece Goods Retailers
records.search_text: Lower-middle-market sewing, quilting, yarn, needlework, and piece-goods retailers with repeat external-customer services such as sewing-machine service coordination, repair, long-arm quilting, paid classes, clubs, block-of-the-month programs, subscriptions, or recurring institutional supply and instruction. Repeat consumer, guild, school, and institutional relationships combining merchandise margin with repair or service fees, classes, clubs, subscriptions, project programs, machine support, or long-arm services; recurring programs often stimulate repeat materials purchases.

records.title: Musical Instrument and Supplies Retailers
records.search_text: US lower-middle-market musical-instrument retailers that provide repeat external-customer services, especially instrument rentals, lessons, repairs, maintenance, school-program support, and associated repeat supplies sales. Households, students, educators, schools, and working musicians buy merchandise and repeat supplies; service-led operators also earn lesson fees, repair charges, and recurring instrument-rental and optional maintenance-plan payments.

records.title: Book Retailers and News Dealers
records.search_text: US lower-middle-market book retailers and news dealers with a transferable repeat-service layer, including recurring institutional or school purchasing and fulfillment, curated subscription programs, paid memberships, managed book clubs, or repeat event and community programs linked to customer revenue. Individuals, member households, schools, libraries, institutions, and community organizations pay for books and related goods; eligible operators add recurring subscriptions or memberships, repeated curated purchasing and fulfillment, or managed program revenue rather than relying solely on episodic transactions.

records.title: Florists
records.search_text: US lower-middle-market florist firms providing repeat outsourced floral design, procurement, assembly, order management, installation, refresh, and delivery for households, weddings and events, funerals, hotels, restaurants, offices, and other external customers. Customers pay fixed order or event prices for flowers, design, assembly, logistics, installation, and service; revenue ranges from occasion orders and event contracts to formal daily, weekly, or monthly floral-refresh agreements.

records.title: Office Supplies and Stationery Retailers
records.search_text: US lower-middle-market firms whose office-products operations include recurring external-customer service: contracted B2B workplace-supplies procurement and replenishment, managed print, recurring copy/print fulfillment, and associated accountable customer service and delivery coordination. Business, public-sector, and education customers buy physical supplies and adjacent products through repeat orders or contracts, with revenue primarily recognized per product delivery; qualifying firms add recurring account management, replenishment, print, fulfillment, or related services. Product margin, delivery economics, and negotiated account pricing dominate rather than a pure recurring software fee.

records.title: Gift, Novelty, and Souvenir Retailers
records.search_text: US lower-middle-market operators that provide recurring outsourced gift-shop or merchandise-program services to attractions, museums, zoos, aquariums, historic sites, hospitality properties, destinations, campuses, airports, or comparable venue partners, including store operation, product development and sourcing, merchandising, staffing, fulfillment, and branded e-commerce. The operator assumes responsibility for a partner venue's retail program and earns product gross margin under a multiyear operating, concession, lease, or revenue-share arrangement. The venue may receive a percentage of gross sales or minimum guarantee, while visitors buy in-store or online merchandise tied to the venue's mission, destination, or brand.

records.title: Used Merchandise Retailers
records.search_text: U.S. lower-middle-market operators in NAICS 459510 that repeatedly perform managed resale or consignment for external sellers, including intake, valuation, merchandising, sale, fulfillment, and seller settlement. The qualifying model earns repeat consignment commissions, seller fees, or a managed-resale spread from households, estates, brands, and institutions while also completing retail transactions with end buyers.

records.title: Pet and Pet Supplies Retailers
records.search_text: U.S. lower-middle-market pet and pet-supply retailers that repeatedly provide external customers with operator-delivered services such as grooming, training, self-wash support, care-plan coordination, or other recurring in-store pet-care workflows alongside merchandise sales. The qualifying firm receives recurring fixed-fee, package, membership, or appointment revenue from pet owners for hands-on or coordinated pet services, often using food and supply retail to acquire and retain the same households.

records.title: Art Dealers
records.search_text: US lower-middle-market art galleries and dealers that repeatedly represent artists, source and broker works, advise collectors or institutions, and execute sales for external customers. Repeat but episodic artist-representation, collector-advisory, consignment and brokerage relationships, monetized principally when artwork is sold through dealer-controlled or fair channels.

records.title: Manufactured (Mobile) Home Dealers
records.search_text: US lower-middle-market manufactured-home dealers that repeatedly serve community operators, developers, government or institutional buyers, or provide repeat outsourced coordination of home selection, permitting, financing, transport, installation, site preparation and post-sale service to external customers. Repeat B2B home sales and dealer-coordination services for community operators and developers, plus service-rich retail transactions where dealer margin and ancillary fees compensate selection, financing, permitting, delivery, installation and site coordination.

records.title: Tobacco, Electronic Cigarette, and Other Smoking Supplies Retailers
records.search_text: Lower-middle-market operators of tobacco, cigar, vape, and smoking-supplies specialty stores or compliant online storefronts whose recurring customer proposition combines assortment, product guidance, age-controlled fulfillment, and repeat replenishment. Predominantly consumer-paid merchandise revenue at posted retail prices, with repeat purchases of consumables and devices; ancillary delivery, loyalty, and product-advice functions support transactions rather than separate fees.

records.title: All Other Miscellaneous Retailers
records.search_text: Lower-middle-market specialty retailers centered on repeat-purchase small-ticket merchandise or recurring custom orders, including art supplies, candles, new collectors' items, trophies and awards, artificial or dried flowers, and recurring pool or spa supplies, sold through stores or owned online storefronts to consumers and external business or institutional customers. Consumer- and business-paid merchandise revenue at posted or quoted prices, with repeat replenishment and occasional customized orders; product selection, light design or configuration, fulfillment, and customer support are bundled into merchandise margin rather than separately billed.

records.title: Line-Haul Railroads
records.search_text: Privately operated U.S. line-haul freight rail services moving cargo over main and branch lines between terminals, with repeat shipper relationships and recurring transportation demand. Freight shippers buy recurring physical transportation capacity through negotiated contracts and tariffs; revenue varies with carloads, lanes, commodity mix, core pricing, service commitments, and fuel-surcharge mechanisms.

records.title: Short Line Railroads
records.search_text: Independently operated U.S. short-line freight railroads providing recurring first-mile, last-mile, switching, storage, and related freight service to external industrial, agricultural, mining, chemical, and logistics customers, within the lower-middle-market EBITDA band. Recurring freight-haul, switching, storage, transloading, and ancillary service revenue paid by shippers or connecting railroads under negotiated contracts, tariffs, and joint-rate arrangements; route-specific physical assets and connections create repeat usage.

records.title: Deep Sea Passenger Transportation
records.search_text: U.S. lower-middle-market operators providing recurring deep-sea passenger or cruise transportation to or from foreign ports for paying external passengers and charter customers. Consumer ticket revenue, charter revenue, and onboard spending for international passenger voyages, sold directly or through travel advisors with advance deposits, dynamic ticket pricing, bundled amenities, and ancillary purchases.

records.title: Postal Service
records.search_text: U.S. private lower-middle-market contractors actually classified in NAICS 491110 that operate contract postal units or perform recurring outsourced mail acceptance, sorting, routing, or delivery within the Postal Service's universal-service system. The Postal Service is the dominant contracting customer and postal patrons are the end users. Contract postal units charge Postal Service prices and receive contract compensation that may be performance-based or fixed; other included contractors earn recurring fees under Postal Service procurement arrangements.

records.title: Newspaper Publishers
records.search_text: United States lower-middle-market newspaper publishers with transferable local, regional, business, or specialist titles and recurring relationships with subscribers, advertisers, sponsors, licensors, or institutional customers across print and digital formats. Readers pay recurring print or digital subscriptions and single-copy charges; advertisers and sponsors buy recurring access to an audience; additional revenue can come from events, content licensing, memberships, and commercial services. The publisher owns or controls the editorial product and distributes it directly or through platforms.

records.title: Periodical Publishers
records.search_text: United States lower-middle-market trade, professional, scholarly, newsletter, and paid specialist periodical publishers with recurring subscriber, advertiser, sponsor, licensing, data, event, or institutional relationships and transferable editorial brands and operations. Readers, professionals, institutions, libraries, and enterprises buy recurring subscriptions, access, licenses, or publication services; advertisers and sponsors buy recurring access to defined audiences; some publishers add events, research, data, custom content, and lead-generation services. The publisher controls the editorial product in print, electronic, or internet-only form.

records.title: Book Publishers
records.search_text: United States lower-middle-market book publishers whose operations include repeat design, editing, production coordination, rights administration, marketing, and distribution of print, electronic, or audio books, with eligibility assessed for recurring or repeat outsourced publishing relationships with external authors, institutions, associations, or content owners. Industry revenue is primarily title and course-material sales through physical and online retail, direct channels, and print, ebook, and audio formats, plus rights and licensing income. The eligible service subset can earn fixed production fees, royalties, revenue shares, or recurring program and list-management fees from authors, institutions, associations, and other content owners.

records.title: Directory and Mailing List Publishers
records.search_text: United States lower-middle-market publishers that repeatedly collect, verify, organize, update, license, rent, or publish directories, mailing lists, databases, and other factual compilations for external advertisers, subscribers, marketers, professional users, or institutions in print or electronic form. Revenue can come from subscriptions, access and download fees, sale or rental of standardized or customer-specified lists, data licenses, advertising in directories, and recurring updates or data services. Customers pay for audience access, structured factual coverage, data freshness, searchability, and compliance-sensitive list management.

records.title: Greeting Card Publishers
records.search_text: U.S. lower-middle-market greeting-card publishers that provide recurring or repeat publishing programs to external retailers, distributors, licensors, or business customers, including seasonal line refreshes, licensed or custom collections, digital catalog production, merchandising content, and repeat replenishment support. Retailers, distributors, licensors, and business customers buy finished card lines, seasonal assortments, private-label or licensed collections, and related digital assets through wholesale orders, repeat programs, royalties, or fixed project and replenishment arrangements. The publisher controls or licenses creative rights and coordinates design, editorial review, production, cataloging, sales, and distribution.

records.title: All Other Publishers
records.search_text: U.S. lower-middle-market all-other publishers that provide recurring or repeat publishing, catalog, licensing, or content programs to external retailers, distributors, institutions, or business customers, including repeat art-print, calendar, reference-artifact, and other nonexcluded print or electronic publishing programs. Retailers, distributors, institutions, licensors, and business customers buy or license copyrighted art prints, calendars, and other published works through wholesale orders, repeat catalogs, seasonal programs, fixed projects, royalties, subscriptions where applicable, or digital licenses. Publishers acquire or create rights, curate editions, prepare content and metadata, market titles, manage customer programs, and coordinate physical or electronic distribution.

records.title: Software Publishers
records.search_text: Commercial software publishers serving external customers through subscriptions, term or perpetual licenses, usage charges, maintenance, support, and related recurring services, viewed at lower-middle-market EBITDA scale. Businesses and consumers pay recurring subscriptions, usage-based charges, license fees, maintenance, support, advertising, or transaction-linked fees for standardized software products. Revenue can be highly recurring, although consumer applications, games, perpetual licenses, and usage-sensitive products are less predictable.

records.title: Radio Broadcasting Stations
records.search_text: Commercial radio broadcasting stations serving external listeners and advertisers through over-the-air programming and station-operated streaming, digital advertising, sponsorship, and related recurring local marketing services, viewed at lower-middle-market EBITDA scale. Stations aggregate listener attention and sell recurring local and national advertising, sponsorship, political advertising, digital marketing, streaming inventory, events, and related services. Revenue is externally paid and often repeat, but cyclical, audience-sensitive, and exposed to advertiser migration across media.

records.title: Television Broadcasting Stations
records.search_text: US lower-middle-market television-station operators selling recurring local advertising and digital campaigns, retransmission or affiliation distribution, and locally produced programming to external audiences, advertisers, and distributors. Recurring and repeat revenue from local and regional advertising, negotiated retransmission consent or affiliation economics, sponsored content, and related digital media services; the accountable operator controls programming, sales, distribution, and compliance.

records.title: Media Streaming Distribution Services, Social Networks, and Other Media Networks and Content Providers
records.search_text: US lower-middle-market niche streaming distributors, social or community networks, and other media network or content-distribution operators that provide recurring audience access, subscription, advertising, syndication, or monetization services to external customers. Recurring subscriptions, advertising and sponsorship, distribution or syndication fees, revenue shares, and recurring creator or publisher monetization services; customers include audiences, advertisers, creators, publishers, and distribution partners.

records.title: Wired Telecommunications Carriers
records.search_text: U.S. lower-middle-market facilities-based wired carriers that repeatedly provide fiber, cable, copper, or mixed-network broadband, voice, video distribution, dedicated internet access, private network, optical transport, wavelength, and wholesale connectivity services to residential, business, carrier, or public-sector customers. Customers generally pay recurring monthly subscription, per-connection, per-location, capacity, or metered bandwidth charges, with additional installation, equipment, usage, support, and wholesale fees. Residential contracts are often cancelable, while enterprise and carrier arrangements may have terms, minimums, service levels, and renewal repricing.

records.title: Wireless Telecommunications Carriers (except Satellite)
records.search_text: U.S. lower-middle-market facilities-based wireless carriers, excluding satellite, that repeatedly provide mobile voice and data, fixed wireless broadband, private or specialized mobile radio, paging, IoT connectivity, and wholesale network access to residential, business, public-sector, or reseller customers. Customers generally pay recurring postpaid or prepaid service charges, account or line fees, data-plan or capacity charges, and wholesale access fees, with additional device, installation, roaming, usage, financing, and support revenue. Plans are frequently bundled and customers can switch, while enterprise, public-safety, wholesale, and private-network contracts may have terms and service levels.

records.title: Telecommunications Resellers
records.search_text: US lower-middle-market mobile virtual network operators, prepaid calling-service resellers, and wired or wireless telecommunications resellers that purchase network access or capacity and repeatedly sell branded telecommunications services to unaffiliated business or household customers without operating the underlying transmission facilities. Customers are households, small and midsize businesses, enterprises, affinity groups, and specialized demographic or usage segments. Revenue is typically recurring subscription, usage, line, seat, or bundled service charges, with economics driven by the spread between customer revenue and wholesale network, interconnection, device, software, support, regulatory, and acquisition costs.

records.title: Agents for Wireless Telecommunications Services
records.search_text: US lower-middle-market independent wireless agent businesses and multi-location cellular or mobile-phone stores that repeatedly sell and activate wireless carrier or reseller service plans on a commission basis for unaffiliated carriers or MVNOs, including related plan advice, upgrades, accessories, device setup, and customer assistance when plan agency is the primary activity. The commissioning customer is a wireless carrier or reseller, while households and small businesses purchase plans and devices through the agent. Revenue is typically activation, upgrade, accessory, device, performance, and sometimes residual commissions or incentives governed by carrier agreements, with repeat economics depending on store traffic, conversion, retention, chargebacks, territory, brand authorization, and ongoing carrier relationships.

records.title: All Other Telecommunications
records.search_text: US lower-middle-market firms providing recurring or repeat non-carrier telecommunications services to external customers, including customer-connection Internet and VoIP services, satellite terminal and tracking operations, communications telemetry, and radar-station operation. Customers pay subscriptions, usage charges, managed-service fees, or repeat operating contracts for voice, connectivity, monitoring, telemetry, tracking, and related communications operations.

records.title: Libraries and Archives
records.search_text: Lower-middle-market independent operators providing recurring or repeat outsourced library, archive, collection-access, research, retrieval, cataloging, digitization, preservation, and records-access services to external customers or members. Eligible operators may earn memberships or subscriptions, recurring institutional contracts, and repeat research, retrieval, digitization, preservation, or collection-management fees. The broader industry also contains appropriation-, grant-, and donation-funded organizations that frequently do not fit the default firm lens.

records.title: Web Search Portals and All Other Information Services
records.search_text: U.S. lower-middle-market firms in NAICS 519290 repeatedly operating niche or general web search portals or providing recurring and repeat information services, including searchable databases, news-clipping services, stock-photo agencies, title-search services other than real estate, and telephone-based recorded information services. Business customers, advertisers, data licensees, and users pay fixed or automatically renewing subscriptions, listing and marketplace packages, data or API licensing fees, usage charges, transaction or referral fees, and impression-, click-, or performance-based advertising; free user access often supplies audience, queries, reviews, or other data that supports monetization.

records.title: Savings Institutions and Other Depository Credit Intermediation
records.search_text: U.S. lower-middle-market savings and loan associations, savings banks, private banks, industrial banks, and similar depository institutions providing recurring deposit, mortgage, consumer, commercial-lending, payment, and account services to external customers. Households and businesses place insured deposits and use loans, payments, and account services; institutions earn net interest spread between loans or securities and funding, plus account and transaction fees, subject to capital, liquidity, consumer-protection, and charter supervision.

records.title: International, Secondary Market, and All Other Nondepository Credit Intermediation
records.search_text: U.S. lower-middle-market firms providing repeat external nondepository credit intermediation through international trade finance, factoring, secondary-market loan acquisition and repackaging, inventory or agricultural credit, industrial lending, pawn loans, and other activities included in NAICS 522299. Borrowers, exporters, importers, sellers of receivables, loan sellers and buyers, and pawn customers pay interest, discounts, origination or servicing fees, pawn service charges, and transaction spreads; secondary-market firms also earn gains, fees, or spreads from acquiring, pooling, servicing, and reselling loans.

records.title: Investment Banking and Securities Intermediation
records.search_text: US lower-middle-market independent broker-dealers, securities brokerages, placement agents, and boutique investment banks that repeatedly provide agency execution, securities distribution, underwriting support, or capital-raising services to unaffiliated issuers and investors for commissions, transaction fees, or success fees. Repeat outsourced intermediation for issuers and investors, monetized through brokerage commissions, transaction fees, placement or underwriting fees, and success-based advisory fees; revenue is relationship-driven and episodic at the mandate level rather than subscription-like.

records.title: Commodity Contracts Intermediation
records.search_text: Lower-middle-market commodity futures, options, spot-commodity, foreign-exchange, and virtual-currency intermediaries that repeatedly act as agents for external customers and earn commissions or transaction fees, including transferable introducing-broker and specialist brokerage operations with ongoing customer, execution, compliance, and reporting responsibilities. Customers include commercial hedgers, producers, institutions, professional traders, and retail accounts. Eligible intermediaries earn commissions, transaction charges, account and platform fees, or disclosed execution economics, while regulated customer onboarding, order handling, supervision, recordkeeping, and support recur with account activity.

records.title: Portfolio Management and Investment Advice
records.search_text: Lower-middle-market portfolio managers, registered investment advisers, wealth managers, financial planners, investment counselors, trust portfolio managers, private-fund managers, and subadvisers repeatedly managing assets or providing customized advice to external clients for fees. Individuals, families, trusts, retirement plans, institutions, and pooled vehicles pay asset-based, fixed, hourly, subscription, and performance fees for discretionary portfolio management, research, reporting, financial planning, and customized investment advice. Revenue commonly recurs with assets and client relationships, while market levels, net flows, mandates, and performance influence fees.

records.title: Pension Funds
records.search_text: No qualifying recurring outsourced-service activity was identified inside the official 525110 definition; the code consists of pension funds, plans, or programs providing retirement income benefits exclusively for a sponsor's employees or members. The classified entity is a benefit fund rather than a fee-earning operator serving external customers. It receives or holds plan resources and pays retirement benefits for the sponsor's employees or members; separable portfolio management and administration are supplied by entities in other industries.

records.title: Health and Welfare Funds
records.search_text: No qualifying recurring outsourced-service activity was identified inside the official 525120 definition; the code consists of funds, plans, or programs providing health- and welfare-related employee benefits exclusively for a sponsor's employees or members. The classified entity is a benefit fund rather than a fee-earning operator serving external customers. It finances or provides medical, surgical, hospital, vacation, training, or other welfare benefits for the sponsor's employees or members; separable portfolio management and claims administration are supplied by entities in other industries.

records.title: Other Insurance Funds
records.search_text: Potentially included only if an establishment correctly classified in NAICS 525190 supplies recurring outsourced services to unrelated external customers. The official definition identifies no such activity: this industry consists of legal entities organized to provide insurance exclusively for a sponsor, firm, employees, or members. The classified entity is a fund or plan serving its sponsor, firm, employees, or members and earns property income rather than revenue from selling services. Any external fee revenue belongs to a differently classified carrier, administrator, or portfolio manager.

records.title: Open-End Investment Funds
records.search_text: Potentially included only if an establishment correctly classified in NAICS 525910 supplies recurring outsourced services to unrelated external customers. The official definition instead identifies legal entities and open-end funds that pool securities or other financial instruments and continuously issue and redeem shares. Investors purchase and redeem fund shares at net asset value; the fund pools assets and earns investment or property income. The investment adviser is typically a separate entity compensated for managing the portfolio, so adviser fee revenue is not the fund vehicle's external-service revenue.

records.title: Trusts, Estates, and Agency Accounts
records.search_text: No operating population survives the default lens: this code contains the trusts, estates, and agency-account legal entities themselves, not firms selling recurring outsourced services. The classified entities hold or administer assets under governing instruments for beneficiaries and earn property income; they do not have external customers buying services. Fee-based fiduciary and portfolio-management providers sit outside this code.

records.title: Other Financial Vehicles
records.search_text: No operating population survives the default lens: this code contains financial legal vehicles and pooled funds, not firms selling recurring outsourced services to external customers. The classified vehicles pool or hold investor assets and generate investment or property income for shareholders or unit holders; they do not have a customer service-revenue model. Professional managers and advisers are separate entities.

records.title: Offices of Notaries
records.search_text: U.S. lower-middle-market offices, including Puerto Rico practices, whose recurring or repeat outsourced work is drafting, approving, executing, receiving, indexing, or storing legal instruments such as real-estate instruments, wills, powers, trusts, and contracts, with the legally empowered notary retaining professional accountability. Individuals, property participants, financial institutions, and recurring professional-referral channels pay statutory or negotiated per-instrument fees plus permissible preparation, research, consultation, certification, and related legal-service fees; repeat business is relationship- and channel-driven rather than subscription-based.

records.title: Offices of Bank Holding Companies
records.search_text: A correctly classified bank holding-company office only if it independently supplies a recurring or repeat outsourced service to unaffiliated external customers while remaining primarily a non-managing holding office. The available classification evidence suggests that this is an exceptional edge case rather than the ordinary establishment model. The default lens requires revenue from repeat services sold to external customers. No defensible recurring external-customer billing model was identified for correctly classified bank holding-company offices; ordinary economics arise from ownership and control of affiliates rather than outsourced-service fees.

records.title: Offices of Other Holding Companies
records.search_text: A correctly classified non-bank holding-company office only if it independently supplies a recurring or repeat outsourced service to unaffiliated external customers while remaining primarily a non-managing holding office. The classification evidence suggests this is an exceptional mixed-activity case rather than the ordinary establishment model. The default lens requires repeat service revenue from external customers. No defensible recurring external-customer billing model was identified for correctly classified other holding-company offices; ordinary economics arise from ownership interests and control rather than outsourced-service contracts.

records.title: Temporary Shelters
records.search_text: U.S. temporary-shelter operators that repeatedly provide short-term emergency or residential shelter to external clients and could plausibly meet the lower-middle-market normalized EBITDA band under the fixed lens. Residents generally are not the economic customer. Funding commonly flows through government grants or subrecipient awards, philanthropy, and institutional sponsorship; a potentially investable subset would require recurring outsourced operating contracts or other reliable third-party payment.

records.title: Sports Teams and Clubs
records.search_text: Professional and semiprofessional U.S. sports teams or clubs in the lower-middle-market band that repeatedly provide live and mediated sports entertainment and associated sponsorship inventory to paying fans, sponsors, and media partners. Recurring season and single-game ticket sales, sponsorship and advertising contracts, local or league-distributed media rights, suites and premium seating, concessions, merchandise and licensing, and league distributions; the mix varies materially by sport and league level.

records.title: Specialized Automotive Repair
records.search_text: US lower-middle-market firms providing repeat specialized mechanical or electrical repair services, including brake, suspension, exhaust, transmission, electrical, and tune-up work, to external vehicle owners and fleet customers. Repair orders paid by consumers, fleets, warranty administrators, or insurers, with revenue from specialist labor, diagnostics, parts markup, and related shop charges.

records.title: Electronic and Precision Equipment Repair and Maintenance
records.search_text: Independent repair and maintenance of consumer electronics, computers, office machines, communications equipment, and scientific, medical, and other electronic or precision instruments without primarily retailing new equipment. Consumer depot work, business service calls, maintenance contracts, warranty subcontracting, field service, calibration, and regulated equipment service generate incident, hourly, fixed-price, and contract revenue.

records.title: One-Hour Photofinishing
records.search_text: Lower-middle-market establishments known as one-hour photofinishing labs that serve external customers by developing still film or making photographic slides, prints, and enlargements on a short-turnaround or while-you-wait basis, including digitally ordered same-day pickup when the establishment's primary activity is photofinishing. Customers pay transparent per-print or per-product prices for rapid physical output, often ordering through a website, app, smartphone, or kiosk before same-day pickup. The model is transaction based, with convenience and local turnaround providing the principal service premium.

records.title: Private Households
records.search_text: The official NAICS 814110 population: U.S. private households directly employing workers on or about the premises for household operation, including nannies, babysitters, cooks, maids, housekeepers, butlers, caretakers, drivers, non-medical aides, private nurses, gardeners, and other yard or maintenance workers. Households are employers and pay employees wages hourly, daily, weekly, or by the job; they may owe payroll taxes and keep employment records. The classified household is not an operating firm selling a recurring outsourced service to external customers, so the screen's customer-revenue and transferable-firm constructs do not apply coherently.

records.title: Executive Offices
records.search_text: U.S. federal, state, county, municipal, and town government establishments serving as offices of chief executives, including presidents, governors, mayors, city and town managers, county executives or supervisors, and their executive advisory committees and commissions. These offices exercise governmental executive authority and provide policy direction, coordination, representation, appointments, budgeting, communications, and constituent-facing administration funded primarily by appropriations and taxes rather than recurring fees from external customers.

records.title: Legislative Bodies
records.search_text: No qualifying lower-middle-market outsourced-service firms were identified; the correctly classified US population consists of government legislative establishments and their advisory committees and commissions. Legislative bodies exercise public authority and are funded through governmental appropriations rather than recurring fees paid by external commercial customers; constituents are represented citizens, not customers under an outsourced-service contract.

records.title: Public Finance Activities
records.search_text: No privately owned firm satisfies the fixed lens. For operational context only, the classified activities are government public-finance functions including tax administration and collection, monetary policy, custody and disbursement of public funds, debt and investment administration, government auditing, and public employee retirement trust administration. The classified establishments exercise statutory public authority and are funded through appropriations, taxes, fees, or public funds. They do not earn recurring outsourced-service revenue from external commercial customers, and their budgets, public benefits, and legal mandates are not transferable EBITDA.

records.title: Executive and Legislative Offices, Combined
records.search_text: Government establishments serving as combined executive and legislative councils, boards of commissioners, or boards of supervisors are retained for operational analysis; no commercial lower-middle-market firm population is included because the NAICS definition is exclusively governmental. These offices exercise public authority for residents and constituents and are funded through appropriated government budgets and public revenues rather than customer contracts, service fees, or normalized EBITDA. Their output includes deliberation, ordinances, policy decisions, hearings, constituent communication, agendas, records, and executive oversight.

records.title: American Indian and Alaska Native Tribal Governments
records.search_text: No private firm population satisfies the fixed lower-middle-market outsourced-service lens. The contextual operating population is federally or otherwise recognized American Indian and Alaska Native governing bodies performing legislative, judicial, administrative, court, and law-enforcement functions for their lands and citizens. Tribal governments exercise sovereign authority and deliver public services to citizens and communities using taxes, fees, gaming or enterprise distributions, grants, compacts, and intergovernmental funding. These are public-finance and government-to-government arrangements rather than commercial outsourced-service contracts with external customers.

records.title: Other General Government Support
records.search_text: Any establishment correctly classified in NAICS 921190 that provides recurring general-government support, including government personnel services, election boards, civil-rights or civil-service commissions, general-services departments, and government supply agencies, and that also satisfies the default external-customer and LMM control-investment requirements. The official industry is composed of government establishments that primarily support government and generally does not have a commercial external-customer revenue model. Any qualifying fringe case would need recurring arm's-length fees from external government customers, but no defensible population of such firms was identified.

records.title: Courts
records.search_text: Civilian federal, state, and local courts of law and sheriffs' offices conducting court functions, assessed operationally; under the fixed acquisition lens there are no qualifying independent private LMM firms because the industry is public administration. Courts provide constitutionally and statutorily mandated adjudication, case administration, records, hearings, orders, and public access, funded principally through government appropriations and partly through prescribed fees rather than recurring outsourced commercial contracts or market pricing.

records.title: Police Protection
records.search_text: U.S. lower-middle-market firms supplying recurring outsourced police-protection services inside NAICS 922120, if any, while exercising the criminal or civil law-enforcement, traffic-safety, or public-order responsibilities described by the code. The fixed lens requires recurring commercial service revenue and normalized EBITDA, but Census defines 922120 as government establishments. No in-code private customer-and-revenue model was identified; adjacent private security and technology suppliers belong to other industries.

records.title: Legal Counsel and Prosecution
records.search_text: No qualifying lower-middle-market firms were identified inside this government-only NAICS classification; government attorney-general, district-attorney, public-defender, public-prosecutor, and government-counsel offices are retained only as operational analogues for the researched workflow primitives. Government offices provide appropriated public legal counsel, prosecution, or defense services rather than recurring outsourced services sold by an eligible firm to external customers; no qualifying commercial revenue model was found within the code.

records.title: Correctional Institutions
records.search_text: Government establishments managing and operating adult or juvenile prisons, jails, detention centers, penitentiaries, and other correctional institutions for confinement, correction, and rehabilitation. Tax-funded and appropriated public correctional operations serving courts and jurisdictions rather than commercial external customers; there is no coherent lower-middle-market private-firm revenue model inside this NAICS code.

records.title: Parole Offices and Probation Offices
records.search_text: U.S. federal, state and local government establishments judicially administering probation and parole offices and boards and pardon boards, including investigation, risk and needs assessment, supervision planning, case contacts, monitoring, court reporting, violation response, referrals, records and administration. These government operations are analyzed only to characterize AI exposure and service durability. Courts, correctional systems and the public authorize and fund these offices through government budgets rather than commercial revenue. Officers supervise people subject to court or parole conditions and provide investigations and recommendations to judicial or executive decision makers. Workload recurs with active caseloads, entries, exits, contacts, monitoring events and statutory reporting, but there is no private revenue model.

records.title: Fire Protection
records.search_text: The industry activity assessed for task and demand context is government firefighting, fire prevention, and combined fire-rescue or ambulance response; under the fixed LMM outsourced-service lens, no establishment qualifies. Government departments deliver tax- and appropriation-funded public safety rather than recurring services to arm's-length customers for commercial revenue; commercial firefighting is classified outside 922160.

records.title: Other Justice, Public Order, and Safety Activities
records.search_text: U.S. government establishments engaged in general administration of public order and safety, public-safety statistics, consumer product safety administration, emergency planning, and disaster preparedness or management, assessed under the fixed lower-middle-market external-service lens. The in-code establishments deliver public administration funded through appropriations, taxes, grants, and intergovernmental transfers rather than recurring commercial fees paid by external customers. Private contractors may supply agencies, but their activity is classified outside this government-only code.

records.title: Administration of Education Programs
records.search_text: No operating establishment in the defined NAICS population qualifies. The fixed lens would require a privately transferable U.S. lower-middle-market firm providing recurring outsourced education-program administration to external customers, while NAICS 923110 is expressly limited to government establishments. Establishments administer public education funds, policy, intergovernmental activity, statistics, data collection, grants, monitoring, and centralized programs under statutory authority and public appropriations, not commercial recurring-service revenue from external customers.

records.title: Administration of Public Health Programs
records.search_text: Government establishments planning, administering, and coordinating public health programs and services, including environmental and mental health programs, health statistics, immunization programs, and public-health inspections. The establishments are government agencies funded through appropriations, taxes, intergovernmental grants, and program-specific public funding rather than recurring commercial fees paid by external customers.

records.title: Administration of Human Resource Programs (except Education, Public Health, and Veterans' Affairs Programs)
records.search_text: U.S. federal, state, and local government establishments planning, administering, and coordinating public-assistance, social-work, welfare, Social Security, disability-insurance, Medicare, Medicaid, unemployment-insurance, workers'-compensation, food-assistance, and related human-resource programs. Government agencies determine eligibility, enroll beneficiaries, calculate and issue or oversee benefits, manage appeals, verify data, prevent improper payments, coordinate programs, and monitor providers or state partners. They are funded through appropriations and taxes rather than recurring commercial fees from external customers.

records.title: Administration of Veterans' Affairs
records.search_text: No qualifying lower-middle-market outsourced-service firms were identified; the correctly classified US population consists of government establishments administering assistance, training, counseling, and related services for veterans and their families or survivors. Veterans' affairs offices administer statutory public programs funded by governmental appropriations rather than recurring fees paid by external commercial customers; veterans and dependents are beneficiaries, not customers under an outsourced-service contract.

records.title: Administration of Air and Water Resource and Solid Waste Management Programs
records.search_text: No privately owned firm satisfies the fixed lens. For operational context only, classified government activities include administration, regulation, enforcement, and intergovernmental coordination of air and water resources, pollution control and prevention, solid and toxic waste, flood control, drainage development, water consumption, and cleanup programs. The classified establishments exercise statutory regulatory and administrative authority funded through appropriations, taxes, grants, or fees. Regulated entities and the public are stakeholders rather than arm's-length outsourced-service customers, and agency budgets or public benefits are not transferable EBITDA.

records.title: Administration of Conservation Programs
records.search_text: Government establishments administering, regulating, supervising, and controlling land use, natural-resource conservation, erosion control, geological survey programs, public and private forest protection, and fish, game, and wildlife conservation are retained for operational analysis; no commercial LMM firm population fits the code. These agencies serve the public, regulated parties, landowners, and other government bodies through appropriated budgets, statutory programs, grants, permits, regulation, monitoring, planning, and stewardship. They do not earn recurring commercial service revenue or normalized EBITDA from external customers.

records.title: Administration of Housing Programs
records.search_text: No private firm population satisfies the fixed lower-middle-market outsourced-service lens. The contextual operating population is federal, state, and local government establishments primarily administering and planning housing programs, including voucher funding, eligibility, payments, compliance, reporting, oversight, and public housing agency planning. Housing-program administrators are funded through appropriations, intergovernmental transfers, grants, and formula-based administrative fees rather than arm's-length commercial service revenue. Housing Choice Voucher administrative fees are tied to leased unit-months and reconciled against appropriated funding and reported program data.

records.title: Administration of Urban Planning and Community and Rural Development
records.search_text: No establishments qualify under the fixed lower-middle-market recurring outsourced-service lens. The NAICS population consists of government establishments administering and planning urban and rural development, including public zoning boards, commissions, and redevelopment agencies; their workflows are still assessed for AI and demand context. The in-code entities are tax- or grant-funded public administrations exercising governmental planning and zoning responsibilities rather than firms billing external customers for recurring services.

records.title: Administration of General Economic Programs
records.search_text: Federal, state, and local government establishments administering, promoting, and developing general economic resources, including economic development, small-business support, tourism promotion, trade commissions, consumer protection, and general economic statistics; under the fixed acquisition lens there are no qualifying independent private LMM firms. Public agencies administer programs, grants, research, statistics, outreach, technical assistance, promotion, and consumer-protection functions for businesses, communities, policymakers, and residents, funded through appropriations and intergovernmental program funds rather than market-priced recurring outsourced contracts.

records.title: Regulation and Administration of Transportation Programs
records.search_text: The frozen lens would include only US lower-middle-market firms classified in NAICS 926120 that repeatedly provide transportation-program administration, regulation, licensing, planning, inspection, or investigation to external customers. The official industry is composed of government establishments funded primarily through appropriations, taxes, user fees, fines, and statutory charges rather than recurring commercial service contracts. No defensible population of private LMM firms with an in-code external-customer revenue model was identified.

records.title: Regulation and Administration of Communications, Electric, Gas, and Other Utilities
records.search_text: No qualifying lower-middle-market firms were identified inside this government-only NAICS classification; public utility commissions and government communications, electric, gas, water, and sewerage regulatory offices are retained only as operational analogues for workflow research. Government offices administer, regulate, license, and inspect utilities through appropriations, assessments, or statutory fees rather than recurring outsourced-service contracts sold by a transferable firm to external customers.

records.title: Regulation of Agricultural Marketing and Commodities
records.search_text: Government establishments planning, administering, and coordinating agricultural production, marketing, and utilization programs, including education, promotion, commodity grading, inspection, quarantine, pest and weed regulation, and enforcement. Tax-, fee-, or appropriation-funded public administration serving producers, consumers, markets, and other agencies rather than commercial external customers; no coherent lower-middle-market private-firm revenue model exists within this NAICS code.

records.title: Regulation, Licensing, and Inspection of Miscellaneous Commercial Sectors
records.search_text: U.S. federal, state, and local government establishments regulating, licensing, permitting, and inspecting miscellaneous commercial sectors, including building and code inspection, professional and business licensing, alcoholic-beverage control, banking, securities and insurance regulation, and enforcement of physical or hazardous-condition standards. The analysis covers the operating work of those public agencies. These are statutory public functions funded primarily through government budgets, sometimes supplemented by license, permit, examination, or penalty revenue. Applicants, licensees, and regulated entities submit applications, renewals, reports, and evidence; agencies perform recurring review, inspection, complaint, investigation, and enforcement cycles. This is not a conventional commercial revenue model.

records.title: Space Research and Technology
records.search_text: The industry activity assessed for task and demand context is government administration and operation of spaceflight, space research, exploration, and space-flight centers; under the fixed LMM outsourced-service lens, no establishment qualifies. Government agencies and flight centers execute appropriated public missions and procure substantial contractor support rather than sell recurring services to arm's-length customers for commercial revenue; private aerospace R&D is classified outside 927110.

records.title: National Security
records.search_text: U.S. government establishments of the Armed Forces and National Guard primarily engaged in national security, including military operations, military police, military courts, military air-traffic control, and military training schools other than service academies, assessed under the fixed external-service lower-middle-market lens. The in-code establishments execute sovereign national-security missions funded through congressional appropriations rather than recurring commercial fees paid by external customers. Contractors sell goods and services to defense agencies but remain classified in their private supplier industries, not NAICS 928110.

records.title: International Affairs
records.search_text: No operating establishment in the defined U.S. NAICS population qualifies. The fixed lens would require a privately transferable lower-middle-market firm supplying recurring outsourced international-affairs services to external customers, while NAICS 928120 comprises establishments of U.S. and foreign governments. Government establishments conduct diplomacy, consular affairs, international assistance, policy, negotiation, representation, analysis, public engagement, and overseas program administration under statutory authority, appropriations, and consular fees rather than commercial recurring-service contracts with external customers.
